text: The surgeon removes the omentum from the anterior surface of the gallbladder and begins dissection to open the peritoneum at the gallbladder's base.

The surgeon identifies the cystic duct with the cystic artery positioned above it. A window is created above the artery, and the gallbladder is partially separated from the liver bed to establish the critical view.

The surgeon releases more of the posterior aspect of the gallbladder from the liver bed, achieving a clear critical view with the cystic duct and artery as the only connecting structures. The surgeon then clips the cystic duct.

The surgeon clips the cystic artery and proceeds to detach the gallbladder from the liver bed. The gallbladder appears thickened and inflamed, with minor bleeding observed due to tearing of the liver capsule during the lifting process.
The surgeon inspects the liver bed, achieving hemostasis. The ducts are clearly visible, including the bifurcation of the common duct into the hepatic ducts.
